examples.title: Test-and-Set Lock Implementation
examples.description: Hardware-based atomic lock using test-and-set instruction
examples.explanation: The test-and-set instruction atomically returns the old value and sets the lock to TRUE. The lock function continuously tests (spins) until the lock becomes available. This provides mutual exclusion but wastes CPU cycles.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average for lock acquisition, O(n) worst case where n is waiting processes

examples.title: Peterson's Solution for Two Processes
examples.description: Software-based mutual exclusion algorithm for two processes
examples.code:
# Shared variables for two processes
# Flag indicates if process wants to enter critical section
# Turn indicates whose turn it is

flag = [False, False]  # flag[0] for P0, flag[1] for P1
turn = 0

def process_0():
    while True:
        # Entry section
        flag[0] = True
        turn = 1
        while flag[1] and turn == 1:
            pass  # Busy wait
        
        # Critical section
        print("Process 0 in critical section")
        
        # Exit section
        flag[0] = False
        
        # Remainder section
        print("Process 0 in remainder section")

def process_1():
    while True:
        # Entry section
        flag[1] = True
        turn = 0
        while flag[0] and turn == 0:
            pass  # Busy wait
        
        # Critical section
        print("Process 1 in critical section")
        
        # Exit section
        flag[1] = False
        
        # Remainder section
        print("Process 1 in remainder section")
examples.explanation: Peterson's solution provides mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting for two processes. It uses two shared variables: flag[] to indicate desire to enter and turn to break ties. Works on systems with sequential memory access but may fail on modern CPUs due to memory reordering.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for entry section in worst case

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation and Usage
examples.description: Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion (mutex) and producer-consumer problem
examples.explanation: Semaphores are synchronization primitives with an integer value and two atomic operations: wait() (decrements and blocks if <= 0) and signal() (increments). Using three semaphores (mutex, full, empty) solves the producer-consumer problem by controlling access and tracking buffer state.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for buffer storage, O(1) for semaphores

examples.title: Compare-and-Swap Lock-Free Algorithm
examples.description: Atomic compare-and-swap instruction for implementing lock-free synchronization
examples.code:
// Compare-and-Swap (CAS) primitive
FUNCTION CAS(location, expected, new_value) -> boolean
BEGIN
    atomic
        IF location.value == expected THEN
            location.value = new_value
            RETURN true
        ELSE
            RETURN false
        ENDIF
    END atomic
END FUNCTION

// Lock-free counter increment
STRUCTURE AtomicCounter
BEGIN
    value : integer
END STRUCTURE

FUNCTION increment(counter : AtomicCounter)
BEGIN
    current : integer
    REPEAT
        current = counter.value
    UNTIL CAS(counter.value, current, current + 1) = true
END FUNCTION

// Explanation: CAS attempts to change value from current to current+1
// If another thread changed it, CAS fails and we retry with new value
// This ensures no race condition on counter increment
examples.explanation: Compare-and-swap atomically compares a memory location with an expected value and swaps it with a new value only if they match. This enables lock-free algorithms where threads retry on conflict rather than blocking. Used extensively in modern concurrent data structures.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized, O(unbounded) worst case with high contention
